summary:
The Dead Sea Scrolls were written and collected primarily between c. 250 BC and 68 AD by the Essene community at Qumran on the northwest shore of the Dead Sea — a Jewish sect that had withdrawn from Jerusalem society in protest against what they saw as the corruption of the Temple priesthood. Hidden in clay jars in caves before the Roman destruction of the community during the First Jewish-Roman War, they were not rediscovered until 1947 when a Bedouin shepherd stumbled upon them.

The scrolls comprise approximately 900 manuscripts: every book of the Hebrew Bible except Esther (in versions a thousand years older than any previously known); sectarian texts describing the community's rules, beliefs, and apocalyptic expectations; biblical commentaries; and previously unknown religious texts. The biblical manuscripts pushed back the known textual history of the Hebrew Bible by roughly a millennium and demonstrated that the text we know today had already been largely stable for over 2,000 years. The sectarian texts revealed a Judaism of far greater diversity and apocalyptic intensity than previously understood — with beliefs about angels, an imminent cosmic battle between 'Children of Light' and 'Children of Darkness,' and a messianic expectation that illuminates the background of early Christianity. The [[event:1149|Dead Sea Scrolls Community]] detail covers the Qumran community's specific practices.
